internal_issues.description: WIN-045 cross-references Section 4.5.4 (Solar Diameter) instead of Section 4.5.2 (Tidal Pattern: One Spike vs. Two Bulges). Same error in WIN-049.
WIN-049 also cross-references Section 4.5.4 instead of Section 4.5.2.
6.6% tidal force variation is the first-order approximation; exact near-far ratio is ~10%. Noting both prevents precision objections.

suggested_patch.find: tidal force varies by only ~6.6% across Earth's diameter
suggested_patch.replace: tidal force varies by only ~6.6% across Earth's diameter (first-order approximation; the exact near-far ratio is ~10%)

internal_issues.description: Doodson (1921) derived 389 harmonic constituents, not 62. The 62 is a common practical subset. Attribution of exactly 62 to Doodson 1921 is imprecise.

suggested_patch.find: derives all 62 constituents, including amplitudes and the two-bulge pattern, from a distant moon.
suggested_patch.replace: derives hundreds of tidal constituents (389 in the full harmonic expansion; 62 is a commonly cited practical subset), including amplitudes and the two-bulge pattern, from a distant moon.

internal_issues.description: Same Doodson 62 precision issue in WIN-050.

suggested_patch.replace: produces all major tidal constituents (389 in the full Doodson expansion; 62 is a commonly cited practical subset)

internal_issues.description: V9 coordinate formula (lat_S = 90 - noon_altitude_june + 23.44) uses globe obliquity as a direct input — smoking-gun evidence that the dome borrows the globe's constants. Not cited in detail_evidence.

suggested_patch.find: The functional form is wrong, not just the parameters.
suggested_patch.replace: The functional form is wrong, not just the parameters. The dome's own V9 coordinate system makes this borrowed dependence explicit: lat_S = 90 − noon_altitude_june + 23.44 — the globe's 23.44° obliquity appears as a direct numerical input for computing latitude from solar observations (raw-text/04-coordinates.txt, line 25). The dome cannot derive this constant from its own geometry; it must borrow it from the spherical model it claims to replace.

internal_issues.description: WIN-005 evidence is thin: one citation and generic NOAA link with no quantified decay rate asymmetry. CHAOS-7 model provides direct quantification.

suggested_patch.find: The westward drift of the Atlantic cell is well-documented by NOAA's continuous monitoring.
suggested_patch.replace: The westward drift of the Atlantic cell is well-documented by NOAA's continuous monitoring. The CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model (Finlay et al., 2020) quantifies the asymmetry: the South Atlantic (Brazilian) cell decays at roughly 160–200 nT/year while the East Indian Ocean cell decays at roughly 80–120 nT/year — a factor-of-two differential that MHD core dynamics reproduce from first principles but that the dome model, lacking any fluid core mechanism, cannot account for.
